segments.text: How many people do you want to come up?
I mean, if feel got more, what more of the team coming up
to the table, then, then do that.
We, we'll probably call
'em up as, okay. That'll
work here. Okay.
Well, um, Ravi, Jeff, team, um, great to have you all here.
Um, thank you very much for the, um, uh, for the draft plan.
Um, much a good read.
Um, we, what we're gonna do is hand it over to you to start
with and, um, then questions I've told, um,
I've asked people where they've got questions.
I said I've got about two hours worth,
but, um, we'll go from there.
Okay. You're the also Thank you. Okay. So over, over to you.
Thank you. So, yeah, uh, I'm ravine chair
of the commission, chief Cooper, CEO.
And thank you for the opportunity.
No one. Thank you. Um, I'll just get onto the draft
National Infrastructure Plan.
Uh, it presents a step towards building
national consensus on New Zealand's infrastructure
challenges we face, uh, well-documented demographic
pressures from an aging population
with infrastructure investment capacity, constrained
by about five to 7% of GDP.
According to current fiscal parameters,
critical knowledge gaps exist in infrastructure,
asset management, government
and partners must improve the understanding of
existing asset condition, lifecycle requirements,
and strategic replacement schedules.
Our strategic approach must prioritize
optimizing existing infrastructure capacity and performance
before permitting resources
to new construction projects.
This will ensure we get maximum value from our current
public investments.
The draft plan delivers transparency
regarding New Zealand's infrastructure needs,
associated costs, and funding pressures.
Systematic improvements in infrastructure, project
delivery is also essential.
Deferred maintenance
and replacement programs have significantly
undermined public trust in New Zealand's infrastructure
delivery capabilities, and threatened future
consensus building efforts.
We have already conducted considerable public
and stakeholder engagement on development of the plan,
including ongoing cross-party briefings.
We expect this to be a useful input
to helping establish greater political consensus
for, for infrastructure.
The delivery of the national infrastructure plan remains the
focus for our organization through to December, 2025,
and will be followed by a government response in mid 2026.
So with that, I'll hand over to Jeff.
Thanks, ravine. Yeah, I just to, I'll just make a couple
of comments, um, and then we can get in into questions.
I think if there's sort of three things I would want you
to reflect on with this plan.
Um, the first is that when we have put forward
what we think is a level of expenditure that we can afford
as a country, we have first
and foremost started with what, um, what it means
to be sympathetic to the household balance sheet, right?
That is where we have started.
We haven't assumed
that there will be new funding tools coming along,
and we've had a long, you know, decades long conversation
about potential new funding tools.
When we see those funding tools change,
we will change our, our forward guidance.
And so we're starting with what we can,
what we can see right now.
We've noted in there that New Zealand currently spends, uh,
among the top 10% o of the OECD,
the amount we spend on infrastructure.
We are also, um, trying to show folks that when it comes
to New Zealand's infrastructure, um,
when we look at it on a per capita level basis,
a a different story actually emerges.
Uh, we are the same size as greater Sydney in terms
of population, but we are spread over a landmass
that's 21 times larger.
When we start comparing our assets, uh, to places like that,
the, the story actually looks a little bit different, right?
So we have sort of six stadiums of capacity over 30,000.
Um, Sydney has five, right?
In New Zealand flies
to about 20 airports around New Zealand.
Sydney has two, two airports, right?
So when you start looking at per capita stocks, uh,
a a slightly different story emerges.
I think that that helps us understand levels
of affordability and what we can expect.
We go further with that to understand
what a new capital budget actually looks like,
a non-renewals, new capital budget, right?
And we, um, the, the detail there is
that New Zealand over a 30 year period has in the order
of $125 billion to spend on new services.
That helps place, uh, why I think it is difficult sometimes
to get big projects across the line, not necessarily
because we can't get political consensus on them, but
because in fact, the trade-offs
of those very large projects, uh, are real
and mean that we can't get services in other areas.
Um, or it might mean that, uh, user charges
and taxes would, would need to be higher to, to do so.
So the first one there is affordability.
The second, uh, that Ravine has already alluded
to is renewals.
We as a commission are very fortunate
to have been directed to think long term.
Um, what that means is that we have to think
through renewal cycles, um, how, uh, cities, communities
are positioned to continually fund the maintenance
and eventual renewal of the services, um,
that they will rely on.
Um, we have said that of every dollar, um,
that we spend on infrastructure, that some 60% should be on,
uh, maintenance and renewals.
Um, in the long run, when, uh, demand drivers are reduced,
uh, that number will tend towards a hundred percent, right?
And my conversations with communities
and areas, uh, that experience less population growth
will tell me that the 60% number is far too low, right?
Um, so in the long run, maintenance is almost everything.
Um, the third thing that I would sort of highlight, um,
before turning over to you, Mr.
Chair, is the role of demographics in the plan.
Demographics are central, uh, to everything in the plan.
They're central to, uh, the demand that we see
for new infrastructure services.
You know, one component of that is more people,
more services and, uh, statistics New Zealand, uh,
released its updated population projections,
actually just a few weeks before we, we released this.
Um, but I think it's worth dwelling on this, that the,
the low to high projections from statistics New Zealand put
New Zealand, uh, at a population of somewhere between six
and a half million and 12 million by 2078.
That is an enormous variation. Mm-hmm. Yeah.
Um, and that really focuses the mind on
what the challenge is for asset owners.
That it's not always clear
where the demand is when it will pop up.
And if you get that decision wrong, it means carrying assets
that can't, can't fund themselves
or have to be funded from other other sources.
And I can give give examples if you're interested,
but the, the, the uncertainty there is real,
and it is what asset owners talk
to us most frequently about.
Uh, we have, um, areas of course that are growing,
but similarly, uh, we have areas that are stagnating
or projected to decline, right?
Uh, this is less front of mind
because growth infrastructure, I think oftentimes
occupies the psyche.
Uh, but 56
of the 67 territorial authorities are expected
to either flatline or decline, uh, into the 2030s.
Uh, so thinking about
how we manage services in a world
where there are less funders around, again, is an area of,
uh, immense concern, I think,
and increasingly will, will become, um, top of mind.
The reason why we are talking about it is
because the assets that we build today oftentimes have, uh,
asset lives of a hundred years that, um, that need
to withstand these demographic changes.
The second part of the demographic story, which, um,
is built into the plan is how we are aging, uh,
and, and start contrast to the uncertainty
that comes about from how much we will grow.
Uh, our aging is far less uncertain.
Um, we will go from a population of, uh,
or from a, um, uh, state
where we have seven working age people
for every retiree in the 1980s to a world at the back end
of this plan, where that number will be two to one.
Um, that of course has enormous implications for, uh,
our fiscal capacity to fund infrastructure.
Um, a point that is made right up front in the strategy.
Um, but it also has wide reaching implications for the types
of services that we require, uh, that relatively speaking,
we will need more infrastructure for older populations
than we will for, uh, for younger populations.
And this is fundamentally what is driving a lot
of the recommendations through the,
for guidance of the plan.
Um, and of course, increasingly will be baked into, um,
business cases, uh,
for infrastructure services in the, in the future.
Um, let me just end by saying that the draft plan is,
um, firstly a draft.
So we're very much in full consultation mode at the moment.
There is a legislative process with the minister, which goes
for three months from, uh, from June 25,
but we're also running a public
consultation period of six weeks.
Um, as, as well, the draft plan
is not one that will sort of sit on, on a,
on a bookshelf and date.
We are looking at this very much
as an operational plan for the commission.
Uh, and the various elements
of the plan we will be updating,
and I'll just quickly go over those.
The Ford guidance, which is our assessment of, uh,
capital investment requirements by sector.
We intend to update, uh, every, every couple of years, um,
as we get better, better information.
Um, the national infrastructure pipeline, which is, uh, uh,
all of the projects that are in planning around the country,
will be updated on a three monthly basis.
We work with about 120 different organizations
around the country to, to reflect, uh, what is in planning,
and then the, uh, infrastructure priorities program, uh,
which is the, uh, menu at our project level of advice and,
and readiness assessment, uh, will be updated one
to two times, uh, a year.
And on those three operational matters, I have, um,
behind me, uh, the directors responsible for those.
So Nadine Dodge is responsible
for the infrastructure priorities program.
Uh, Mac Kerr is the director of data science
and analytics who runs the national
infrastructure, um, pipeline.
And, um, uh, Graham Campbell, uh, the director of, uh,
research and economics runs the Ford Guidance
Program, uh, as well.
And John Butler we have here who's been coordinating,
um, the, the plan.
So with that, I'm happy to hand back to you, Mr. Chair.
Thank you both. And, um, thank you for such free
and frank, um, uh, advice to start us off with.
Um, I'm gonna keep, we've try
and keep the questions quite tight if we can,
just picking up on the first one of 'em,
because as you've come before and, um,
we're talking about demographics
and how central those are to the plan, I've sort of said
for 40 years we should have a
demographic strategy of some sort.
Um, how,
If we Said, I mean, that range, you've got between sort
of less than 5 million
and 12 million, um, in terms of being able to plan anything
for infrastructure without some sort of greater degree
of certainty, how difficult is that?
And secondly, how much of what we are pro what
what you are thinking about in terms of going forward.
'cause you talk about 125 billion to spend on new services
that, well, that will obviously depend on
how many people you're, you're dealing with,
whether it's 5 million
or 12 million or something in between.
Um, what proportion of, um,
expenditure do you see being necessary for growth?
Yeah, if we are five, presumably virtually none, um,
if we're at 12, presumably it's a heck of a lot.
But how do we, yeah. How do we work on that, that sort of,
Yeah. So, um,
on, on your, on your first point around, um,
uncertainty, um, there, as, as I, as I said
before, this is like front
and center for, for all, all asset owners,
there are different types of uncertainty, right?
Um, and I would sort of differentiate between two,
particularly the first is a question of timing.
When is the growth going to come, um, uh, such
that we might need a service?
Um, and so, uh, you know, I'll take the case of Auckland
where historically our population projections have been
for the last 20 years.
It's not true overall in fact,
but just for the last 20 years have been, um, undershooting.
Where Auckland has got to
this essentially is challenging asset owners
to figure out when is the right time
to bring the asset along.
It is fundamentally a financing challenge
because if you bring the asset on, uh,
earlier than perhaps you, you should, according to demand,
it means you might need to hold debt
for a little bit longer, right?
So you've got a financing risk there.
The second type of problem is if you get the
direction wrong, right?
So rather than growing your, uh,
community actually starts contracting, right?
And this is a much more serious problem.
This is not a financing problem.
This becomes a funding problem.
Uh, and if you have a funding problem, it means
that the users aren't turning up to use the asset, right?
Um, this is one that asset owners worry about far,
far more in, in many ways.
Um, so there are different types of uncertainty in,
in different, um, sectors.
Uh, I would point to something like
where we see the most uncertainty is an area like, uh,
corrections where our ability to forecast demand is
not to be too flippant, like pretty hopeless.
We get the direction wrong all the time.
So it's a, it is a type two problem.
Um, uh, for for many other types of infrastructure,
I would take something like, um, electricity where, uh,
MBS projections have been wrong on the downside
for a decade or or so.
Um, uh, you know, that, that is a, a sector that's, um,
that know the actual demand
for electricity has been relatively flat.
So what to do in those situations.
Um, I think where we point to in the plan is that buying,
if you're not sure when demand is gonna come,
buying options is better than buying assets.
Um, leave the option to buy the asset,
but do some of the initial work so
that when the demand does come,
you can turn it around quickly.
Um, to give you a concrete example of what this looks like,
like land protection, right?
Don't wait until moments
before you decide you need the asset to go and buy the land.
Um, buy the land, buy yourself some
options, um, before that.
Um, so that the implementing those strategies
of investment under uncertainty is, is critical
for, for infrastructure.
Um, your second question is about, uh,
how our advice would change under different,
particularly population growth estimates.
We have, um, put forward a, uh,
a expenditure number as a proportion of GDP, um, which
of course is, is tied to, um, population growth.
So if the, if the, if the population were to grow less,
obviously the capital budget would be less,
but our advice of the proportion
of GDP would remain constant,
and that's actually why we've chosen that
as a metric rather than, um, front footing with,
with headline numbers, notwithstanding the fact
that I gave you the, the headline number under
the median just before,
Um, I don't see any other hand.
Okay. I'll go to Julianne.
Thanks so much. Um, I had a couple
of different areas of questions.
Maybe if other people have questions, like
You can take one and then Yeah, yeah.
Then unless there's in video else, I'll go one,

some examples of what using our

And, um, and I had a question about

Um, one of the recommendations

Would that be true of all types
of projects or just certain projects?
Because, um, given
that there isn't full pricing for time of use

in most places there's some transport subsidy needed
for public transport.
Um, I wouldn't think that investment in some big,
well, some improvements
to public transport could be funded from
user charges, but mm-hmm.
Yeah. So I was just wondering if that is applies
to all transport or just certain projects?
Yeah. Yeah. So the advice, maybe I'll start with that one
and then I'll, I'll come back to the, um, making better use
of existing infrastructure.
Um, where we are going with on that is that, uh,
the, the, the, that the network itself should be able to pay
for it for itself now, except in,
in transportation there's questions of what networks, right?
Freight networks, um, uh, eroding networks,
CT networks, and so on.
Um, what, what we're saying here is that as a starting point
that everyone using that network should sub, should,
notwithstanding the fact
that there might be cross subsidies within the network.
And I would, I think this is an important point.
So, um,
if you think about the way electric transmission pricing is
done, there are cross subsidies across the network,
but the network is a whole pace for itself.
Um, it is, it is a lower bar than the bar
that has held up to say, um, the airlines industry and the,
and the airlines industry.
Um, the bar is much higher.
Uh, there we say, uh, essentially if you look at jet stars,
um, uh, route coverage, um, you know,
they will probably not extend new routes
unless they can get a profit above zero,
um, on that new route.
So for them, each part of the network needs
to have a profit above zero.
Um, what we are holding up for transport is less than that.
That the reason why we think that this is important is
because it creates pricing tension within the sector.
Uh, and one of the most notable things about the
transportation sector is that on a per capita basis, um,
we have been spending, um, enormous, um, uh,
amounts on transportation, uh,
over the last two, two decades.
I think there is a, um, a chart in your pack comparing, uh,
electricity per capita, um, spend to transport spend.
Mm-hmm. I, from the top of my head,
I think the per capita number
for electricity is in the order of 50%,
and for transportation at per capita number is
400% increase.
So we've seen a very large increase in transportation.
At the same time, we have seen a sort of breaking
of the ated model, uh, in transportation,
and that essentially has, yeah, that's the one there, yeah.
Has reduced the sort of pricing tension on the sector, um,
uh, to minimize capital expenditure.
So part of this pricing, um, advice is,
is about right sizing the capital,
the perceived capital investment requirements
for the sector, so that that areas
of investment that have no chance
of funding themselves off the asset base can still occur.
And I think that that's really I important part
of this advice is that, yes, there is a, um, part
of the plan that is saying we need
to tighten up the user charger elements of, particularly
for networked infrastructure,
but the motivation for doing that is to release funding
for social infrastructure
because funding, um, infrastructure like hospitals
and schools, um, correctional facilities, justice
and so on, these are areas of the system
that will never fund themselves.
Um, and so by tightening up the areas that can we,
we release, uh, an ability to,
to hit the infrastructure we need in the social space.
Um, on your question about, um, how we go about doing that,
I think that the pricing instruments in transportation are,
um, you know, when you stack them up against
how we do it in electricity, are found wanting, you know,
we don't have congestion charging, um, in place.
The time of day stuff looks far more important.
We're considering that later on. Yeah. Yeah.
So, you know, so these are things that, um,
we think will make a big, big difference, um, uh, in,
in constraining the, the capital side of things as well.
Um, does that answer
Yeah, if, can I just do one follow up
On if, if it's a quick one?

if you build this, there will be economic benefits,

And, um, there's, yeah.
Yeah. Um, I think one of the answers to this

um, and being able to get through the renewal cycle.
Um,
ultimately, you know, you want to build assets

that the users are then able to pay for the ongoing upkeep

So I think in the, in the plan,
we asked this question noting
that there are fiscal constraints.
I think a natural question is to ask, well, when can we go
outside of those fiscal constraints?
Because the truth is those fiscal constraints look actually,
um, they're quite confronting.
Mm-hmm. Um, uh, when you look over a a 30 year period,
I mean, we are running structural deficits that get us
to a number of 150% net core crown debt to GDP.
I think that's concerning. Um, there's obviously some policy
questions there that go outside the, the realm of, um,
of infrastructure, but we look at that and
and say, well, if that's the case, trying to hit our numbers

and 7% of GDP actually looks pretty
challenging in that context.
A natural question is to say, well, how can we go
outside of those numbers?
And there's many different types of infrastructure that
actually will not be flustered
by those fiscal constraints at all.
Um, regulated utilities have their own, um,
have their own funders.
And so, uh, you know,
if they can put forward high quality projects
that people are prepared to pay for, actually that doesn't,
the fiscal stuff doesn't matter so much, um, to that.
Um, so when it comes to road to, to transportation, um,
the, clearly there is a much tighter link between, um,
the asset and, um, fiscal constraints more, more generally,
which I think become relevant.
So our advice to this is if you want to go outside of those,
um, those fiscal constraints in the first instance,
you could do it, uh, within, um, base load,
but it means, it probably means getting less social
infrastructure, at least you wanna raise
taxes, and that's the choice.
Um, if you want to go outside of that
and do, um, uh, say an economic PPP
or something that where the asset is gonna pay
for itself when it comes to, uh, the, uh,
the roading example, but more generally the process,
we wanna show, what would you need to see to believe
that this road, uh,
or asset is gonna be able to pay for itself.
Um, and in the case of roading infrastructure, we show that,
that we would need to see costs, uh, that are
below $32 million per kilometer usage that's
above 40,000 users per day.
Um, and, uh, what's the last one?
Cost, usage, and thank you.
And travel time, which is, um, greater than 15 minutes.
If you manage to hit those three things, your, um,
the willingness to pay the toll will cover the asset, um,
and the ongoing maintenance costs,
and it gets you outside of your fiscal constraints.
So I think that's the question you can ask is if,
if this is true, not withstanding the initial stimulus,

The, in the initial stimulus comes and goes.

Uh, and if you don't have enough users,

where I would sort of go to on, uh, the,

That if we can demonstrate we have good projects

of a sudden, um, the funding looks kind of different.

Um, and if we're trying to, to put forward projects

The infrastructure priorities program,

So, um, a agencies, the private sector elect

Um, we then, um, uh, well,
there's a triage point of this, so it has

So we're, we're sort of, we're not doing the sort of 10
and $20 million ones.
These are the sort of more, more big projects.

independent process, right?
So the business cases will come to us, uh, we will either
evaluate them internally or in some cases,
if we don't have the expertise, we will, um,
get an external reviewer.
That appraisal will then go to an independent group of five,

with the evaluation framework we set out
and is published on our website.
Uh, and we'll assess the project against, um,
three criteria, strategic alignment value for money, um,
and deliverability, uh, and, uh,
and then give it a, a stage of readiness, right?
Stage one being, yep, we agree you've got a problem,
but we don't think you've got a solution just yet.

and you've got a range of potential solutions.

Briefing on the National Infrastructure Plan
